risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomly allocated to a waiting list or MBSR using computer-generated random numbers in a permuted block design with randomly varying block lengths. Allocations were stratified by type 1 or type 2 diabetes and by pain severity..." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random method (computer-generated random numbers) with additional safeguards (permuted blocks, varying block lengths, stratification), so the allocation sequence was truly random.

Quote: "Allocations were generated by an independent statistician and concealed from investigators and treating physicians. Treatment allocation occurred as close as possible to the start of the MBSR course when the next consecutively numbered opaque envelope for the appropriate strata was opened." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment mechanism that prevented foreknowledge of assignment: the allocation list was concealed, and the next consecutively numbered opaque envelope was opened only at allocation time. Although the passage does not say "sealed," the report explicitly states the allocations were concealed and uses consecutive numbered opaque envelopes, which is sufficient here to infer allocation concealment was adequate.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomly allocated to a waiting list or MBSR" and "Patients in the control group were offered enrollment in an MBSR course after the study was complete." Comment: This was an open wait-list controlled behavioral intervention; participants and personnel delivering MBSR would necessarily know allocation, and the outcomes included self-reported pain, depression, stress, and quality of life that could plausibly be influenced by that knowledge.

Quote: "After randomization, participants had had sufficient experience with the instruments that they were able to complete them without any contact from study staff who were aware of the treatment allocation." Comment: Most key outcomes were patient self-report measures (e.g., BPI, PHQ-9, PGIC, PSS, SF-12, NeuroQoL, PCS, self-care activities), so the patient was effectively the outcome assessor. However, the paper does not explicitly state that participants were blinded, and the statement only indicates that staff were aware of allocation while participants completed questionnaires without staff contact. Although A1C is objective, the study reports a mix of subjective and objective outcomes, so without explicit participant/outcome-assessor blinding the overall detection bias judgment is unclear.

Quote: "Of the 33 subjects allocated to MBSR, 31 received the intervention and completed follow-up, and 30 were included in the analysis. ... Of the 33 patients allocated to the control group, one withdrew from the study, and 32 completed follow-up and analysis." Comment: Attrition was very low and broadly balanced (MBSR 3/33 not analyzed, control 1/33 withdrew; overall 4/66 = 6.1%). The reasons given are not outcome-related except for one ineligible participant who was excluded after randomization, and the paper states "Patient retention was excellent, with 94% of those randomized included in the analysis," so missing data are unlikely to bias the results.
